subject: ap-precalculus
title: Exponential Growth Modeling: Bacterial Population, Competing Models & Semi-Log Analysis
stimulus:
A biologist is studying a colony of bacteria growing in a nutrient-rich solution. The biologist measures the population at selected times and records the following data:

| Time $t$ (hours) | Population $P$ (thousands) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 3.1 |
| 2 | 7.4 |
| 5 | 28.6 |
| 8 | 110.3 |
| 11 | 421.7 |

The biologist considers two competing models for the population: an exponential model $E(t) = a \cdot b^t$ and a power model $W(t) = c \cdot t^d$.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 11$ to write two equations that can be used to find the values of constants $a$ and $b$ in the exponential model $E(t) = a \cdot b^t$.

(ii) Find the values of $a$ and $b$ as decimal approximations. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: First equation gives $a = 3.1$ directly. For $b$: $3.1 \cdot b^{11} = 421.7 \Rightarrow b = (421.7/3.1)^{1/11} = 136.03^{1/11} \approx 1.563$ (accept $[1.558, 1.568]$). Supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step. A student using a different data pair will get a slightly different $b$; accept any two-point derivation with correct algebra.

scoring_points.description: Two correct equations: $a \cdot b^0 = 3.1$ (giving $a = 3.1$) and $3.1 \cdot b^{11} = 421.7$
wrong_examples: Using $a = 0$ or $b = 0$ (not consistent with exponential growth)
common_traps: $b^0 = 1$ for any base $b$, so the first equation immediately gives $a = 3.1$
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for writing two correct equations using $t = 0$ and $t = 11$: $a = 3.1$ (from $b^0 = 1$) and $3.1 \cdot b^{11} = 421.7$. Writing only one equation or using incorrect data points does not earn the point.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies both equations from the specified data points, using the zero-exponent property to immediately determine $a = 3.1$.
Writes only one of the two required equations. Both the $t = 0$ and $t = 11$ equations are needed.
Uses $t = 8$ instead of $t = 11$ for the second equation. The problem specifies using $t = 0$ and $t = 11$.

scoring_points.description: Correct values $a = 3.1$ and $b \approx 1.563$ from $b = (421.7/3.1)^{1/11}$; supporting work must show the root-taking or logarithm step
common_traps: $b^{11} = 136.03$ must be solved for $b$ by taking the 11th root — not leaving it as $b = 136.03$
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for $a = 3.1$ and $b \approx 1.563$ (accept $[1.558, 1.568]$) with supporting work showing the root-taking or logarithm step. Reporting $b = 136.03$ (the ratio before taking the root) or a value with fewer than three decimal places does not earn the point.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly divides to isolate $b^{11}$, then takes the $11$th root and reports $b$ to three decimal places.
Computes the ratio $421.7/3.1$ but does not take the $11$th root. The value $136.03$ is $b^{11}$, not $b$.
Correct method but reports $b$ to only two decimal places; this is a decimal presentation error and does not earn the point on its first occurrence.

parts.prompt:
(i) To determine whether the exponential model $E$ is more appropriate than a linear model for this data, a biologist creates a semi-log plot by graphing $\ln(P)$ versus $t$. Using the data at $t = 0$ and $t = 11$, calculate the slope of the line on the semi-log plot.

(ii) Use the exponential model $E(t) = 3.1 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a) to estimate the population at $t = 6$ hours. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), slope $= (\ln(421.7) - \ln(3.1))/(11 - 0) \approx 4.912/11 \approx 0.447$ (accept $[0.444, 0.449]$). This equals $\ln(b)$ from part (a). Supporting work must show the quotient using $\ln$ of the population values. For part (ii), $E(6) = 3.1 \cdot (1.563)^6 \approx 42.7$ thousand (accept $[40, 46]$ to account for rounding differences in $b$). Supporting work must show substitution of $t = 6$.

scoring_points.description: Correct slope $\frac{\ln(421.7) - \ln(3.1)}{11} \approx 0.447$; supporting work must show a quotient using $\ln$ of the population values
common_traps: On a semi-log plot, the slope is $\frac{\Delta \ln P}{\Delta t}$ — must use $\ln$ of the data values in the numerator
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for the correct slope $\approx 0.447$ (accept $[0.444, 0.449]$) with supporting work showing the quotient $\frac{\ln(421.7) - \ln(3.1)}{11}$. Using raw population values instead of their natural logarithms does not earn the point.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly uses the natural logarithm of population values in the numerator, and the time interval in the denominator, arriving at the semi-log slope.
Uses raw population values instead of their natural logarithms. On a semi-log plot the vertical axis is $\ln P$, so logarithms must be used in the slope calculation.
Computes the numerator correctly but omits dividing by $\Delta t = 11$. The slope must be a rate (change in $\ln P$ per unit time).

scoring_points.description: Correct estimate $E(6) \approx 42.7$ thousand using the student's $b$ value from part (a); supporting work must show substitution of $t = 6$ into the model
wrong_examples: Using a $b$ value not derived from part (a)
common_traps: The model is $E(t) = 3.1 \cdot b^t$, not $3.1 \cdot b \cdot t$ — exponentiation required, not multiplication by $t$
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for an estimate consistent with the student's $b$ from part (a), obtained by substituting $t = 6$ into $E(t) = 3.1 \cdot b^t$. Accept answers in $[40, 46]$ thousand to account for rounding differences in $b$. Supporting work must show the substitution.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly substitutes $t = 6$ into the model, computes the exponential, and reports the result in thousands consistent with the $b$ value from part (a).
Multiplies $b$ by $t$ instead of raising $b$ to the power $t$. The model uses exponentiation: $E(t) = 3.1 \cdot b^t$.

parts.prompt:
(i) The biologist's colleague proposes that the population exceeds 500 thousand bacteria sometime between $t = 11$ and $t = 12$ hours. Using the exponential model $E(t) = 3.1 \cdot b^t$ with $b$ from part (a), write and solve an inequality to find the earliest time $t$ at which the population first exceeds 500 thousand. Express your answer as a decimal approximation.

(ii) The colleague also suggests a linear model $L(t) = mt + 3.1$ fitted through $t = 0$ and $t = 11$. Find the value of $m$ as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), $3.1 \cdot b^t > 500 \Rightarrow b^t > 500/3.1 \Rightarrow t > \ln(500/3.1)/\ln(b) \approx \ln(161.29)/\ln(1.563) \approx 5.084/0.447 \approx 11.38$ hours (accept $[11.3, 11.5]$ depending on $b$). Supporting work must show the inequality setup and logarithm step. For part (ii), slope $m = (421.7 - 3.1)/(11 - 0) = 418.6/11 \approx 38.055$ (accept $[38.0, 38.1]$). Supporting work must show the difference quotient.

scoring_points.description: Correct inequality setup and solution $t \approx 11.38$ hours; supporting work must show the inequality and logarithm step
common_traps: Since $\ln(b) > 0$ (because $b > 1$), dividing by $\ln(b)$ preserves the inequality direction — the answer $t > 11.38$ is correct
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for the correct solution $t \approx 11.38$ hours (accept $[11.3, 11.5]$) with supporting work showing the inequality setup $3.1 \cdot b^t > 500$ and the logarithm step. Omitting the logarithm step or the inequality direction does not earn the point.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly sets up the inequality, takes logarithms, divides by $\ln(b) > 0$ (preserving direction), and arrives at $t > 11.38$ hours.
Solves for the equality (when population equals $500$) but does not write or conclude with the inequality $t > 11.38$. The question asks for the earliest time the population first exceeds $500$.

scoring_points.description: Correct slope $m = \frac{421.7 - 3.1}{11} \approx 38.055$; supporting work must show the difference quotient using the data values
common_traps: The slope formula requires the change in $P$ over the change in $t$: $(P_{11} - P_0)/(11 - 0) = (421.7 - 3.1)/11$
scoring_points.official_rubric: The point is earned for the correct slope $m \approx 38.055$ (accept $[38.0, 38.1]$) with supporting work showing the difference quotient $\frac{421.7 - 3.1}{11 - 0}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the slope formula using the two specified data points $(0, 3.1)$ and $(11, 421.7)$ and computes the result to three decimal places.
Uses $421.7/11$ without subtracting the initial value $3.1$. The slope must be the change in $P$ divided by the change in $t$: $(421.7 - 3.1)/11$.
Adds the population values instead of subtracting them. The slope formula uses the difference $P_{11} - P_0$, not the sum.
